Foundations and Scalar Fields.topic: Classical field theory and Lagrangian formalism
Foundations and Scalar Fields.description: Build field theories from symmetry principles using actions and Euler–Lagrange equations. Connect kinetic and interaction terms to conservation laws and dimensions.
Derive equations of motion for scalar or vector fields, including derivative couplings. Track overall signs and coefficients carefully and interpret terms physically.
Relate continuous symmetries to conserved currents and charges. Distinguish internal vs spacetime symmetries in simple models and extract explicit currents.
Construct stress tensors and test conservation. Examine traceless limits and symmetry under Lorentz transformations.
Classify fields by spin and transformation properties. Verify covariance of actions and bilinears using indices or gamma-matrix conventions at a conceptual level.
Promote classical fields to operators, impose commutators, and define creation/annihilation operators. Connect normalizations to delta functions and box quantization.
Analyze Fourier modes, on-shell relations, and normalization for free relativistic fields. Relate to physical energies and group velocity.
Compute spacelike (anti)commutators and identify conditions for microcausality. Clarify locality assumptions.

Path Integrals and Quantization.description: Formulate generating functionals for free theories and relate functional derivatives to correlation functions. Track signs and normalization.
Evaluate quadratic path integrals and determinants. Connect to propagators through completing the square and inverses of operators.
Relate Z, W, and Γ. Distinguish connected, amputated, and 1PI objects and their roles in perturbation theory.
Organize time evolution with interactions and set up the Dyson expansion. Use interaction-picture operators and time ordering consistently.
Construct time-ordered, retarded, and advanced propagators in x- and p-space. Emphasize boundary conditions and mass dependence.
Reduce time-ordered products to contractions and normal-ordered terms. Connect operator algebra to diagrammatics.

Symmetries and Scattering.description: Generate Feynman rules and compute elementary tree amplitudes. Track symmetry factors and identical-particle combinatorics.
Study 2→2 scalar scattering, channels, and basic kinematics. Compare identical vs distinguishable cases and symmetry factors.

Symmetries and Scattering.topic: Cross sections and Lorentz-invariant phase space
Symmetries and Scattering.description: Relate matrix elements to observables using flux and LI phase space. Explore threshold behavior and high-energy limits.
Connect amputated Green’s functions to S-matrix elements and clarify wavefunction renormalization. Focus on which factors appear and why.
Contrast U(1) symmetry, charge, and particle content. Derive the Noether current for complex fields.

Symmetries and Scattering.topic: Discrete symmetries (P, C, T) for scalars
Symmetries and Scattering.description: Classify simple operators under parity, charge conjugation, and time reversal. Provide selection rules in basic processes.
Decompose simple scattering amplitudes into partial waves. Identify s- and p-wave dominance near threshold.
Relate forward amplitude to total cross section in simple models. Check unitarity constraints qualitatively.
Use the Källén λ function to handle two-body kinematics. Analyze allowed regions and special limits.
Track mass dimensions of fields and couplings. Use natural units to check consistency of results.
Identify conditions for vacuum stability in polynomial scalar potentials. Classify phases and small oscillations.

Fermions and Spinor Structure.description: Solve plane-wave spinors and completeness relations; compute bilinears and currents. Interpret helicity and chirality in simple processes.
Build fermionic Fock space from anti-commutation relations. Connect field operators to particle number and charges.
Use Clifford algebra identities and trace technology. Classify bilinears by Lorentz and discrete symmetries.
Work with chiral projectors, massless limits, and spin sums. Apply to scattering and symmetry analyses.
Classify fermionic operators under P, C, and T. Provide selection rules and simple examples.

Gauge Fields and Symmetry.description: Derive local U(1) invariance, Feynman rules, and Ward identities. Examine gauge choices and physical observables.
Formulate non-Abelian field strengths and covariant derivatives. Understand self-interactions and color factors.
Quantize non-Abelian theories in covariant gauges. Derive ghost terms, vertices, and their role in loops.
Relate symmetries to Green’s function identities. Constrain renormalization and amplitude structures.
Compare gauge-fixing strategies and their impact on intermediate quantities. Emphasize gauge-parameter independence of observables.

Renormalization and Quantum Corrections.description: Analyze photon self-energy at one loop and momentum dependence of propagators. Interpret charge screening in QED.
Regulate loop integrals in d=4−2ε. Track poles, scales, and scheme conventions (MS/ℳS̄).
Define renormalization conditions, Z-factors, and parameter redefinitions. Compare on-shell vs minimal schemes.
Derive coupling evolution with scale and interpret effective charge. Connect to physical cross sections.
Compute 2- and 4-point divergences and extract Z-factors. Discuss coupling running and triviality qualitatively.

Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking and Higgs.description: Connect continuous symmetry breaking to massless modes via current algebra. Provide simple examples.
Show gauge boson mass generation via SSB and gauge fixing. Track eaten modes and physical spectra.
Extend to SU(2)-like settings with multiple gauge bosons. Emphasize counting of degrees of freedom and masses.
Link forward scattering to total cross sections. Verify S-matrix consistency at tree or one-loop level in simple channels.
Understand soft/collinear singularities and their cancellation in inclusive rates. Use soft factors and KLN intuition.

Applied Topics and Tools.description: Express two-point functions via spectral densities and discuss positivity/normalization constraints.
Relate real/imaginary parts of amplitudes through analyticity and subtractions. Apply to simple correlators.
Use partial-wave unitarity to constrain couplings or cross sections in toy models.
Relate s-, t-, and u-channel processes through analytic continuation for simple amplitudes.
Connect field renormalization factors to residues and asymptotic states for calculable examples.

Renormalization Group and Scaling.description: Formulate RG equations and classify fixed points. Linearize flows and extract simple critical exponents.

Renormalization Group and Scaling.topic: β functions in φ⁴ and QED
Renormalization Group and Scaling.description: Compute one-loop β-functions with attention to factors and schemes. Interpret UV/IR behavior and triviality vs asymptotic freedom.
Determine field/operator rescaling with scale and relate to correlation function scaling.
Explain how dimensionless couplings generate a physical scale through renormalization; connect to running couplings.
Relate RG flow to scale dependence of correlators and Green’s functions. Solve simple CS equations.

Non-Abelian Gauge Theories.topic: Non-Abelian renormalization and QCD β function
Non-Abelian Gauge Theories.description: Evaluate color factors and derive asymptotic freedom for SU(N). Discuss nf-dependence and scheme issues.
Preserve gauge invariance in effective actions and streamline RG calculations. Emphasize background vs quantum fields.
Use Wilson loops to diagnose confinement. Contrast area vs perimeter behaviors and simple models.
Introduce BRST transformations and cohomology ideas. Connect to gauge fixing and ghost structure.

Finite Temperature and Vacuum Effects.description: Use imaginary time and periodicity to compute thermal correlators. Perform simple Matsubara sums.
Study symmetry restoration/breaking at finite temperature using simple scalar models and high-T expansions.
Evaluate vacuum energy shifts due to boundaries in simple geometries. Interpret forces and scaling.

Anomalies and Supersymmetry.description: Compute axial anomalies and understand current non-conservation. State cancellation conditions.
Identify gauge anomaly structures and cancellation criteria in simple fermion spectra.
Construct bounce solutions and estimate vacuum decay rates semiclassically. Track determinants conceptually.
Build supercharges and superfields; derive component actions. Verify invariance and multiplet structure.
Construct the simplest interacting SUSY model and analyze component interactions and invariants.

Amplitude and On-Shell Methods.description: Represent external states with spinor variables and express amplitudes using angle/square brackets. Enforce little-group scaling and on-shell constraints.
Separate color from kinematics in non-Abelian amplitudes using trace or color-flow bases. Compare partial amplitudes across bases.
Reconstruct tree amplitudes from complex momentum shifts and factorization. Address boundary terms and valid shifts.
Connect imaginary parts of amplitudes to sums over on-shell intermediate states. Apply to forward scattering and check consistency.

Loop and Regularization Techniques.description: Reduce tensor integrals to scalar masters and organize loop computations systematically. Track dimensional factors consistently.
Evaluate d-dimensional integrals and keep ε-poles and finite remainders straight. Clarify scheme dependence in results.
Use IBP relations to reduce loop integrals to masters. Understand Laporta-style reduction conceptually.

Loop and Regularization Techniques.topic: Feynman parameters and simple master integrals
Loop and Regularization Techniques.description: Parametrize loop integrals and evaluate standard bubbles/triangles/boxes in simple kinematics.

Effective Field Theories.description: Organize short-distance expansions and Wilson coefficients for correlators and currents. Treat running and mixing where needed.
Derive low-energy effective actions by eliminating heavy fields. Compute threshold corrections and power counting.
Build the chiral Lagrangian under symmetry constraints and compute low-energy pion processes. Track LECs and orders.
Factorize hard/collinear/soft modes and resum large logarithms. Outline matching and running at a conceptual level.
Use heavy-quark symmetry and 1/m expansions for heavy–light hadrons and currents. Identify spin/flavor symmetry predictions.
Describe quarkonium with nonrelativistic expansions and leading bound-state effects. Contrast with potential models at a high level.
Evolve Wilson coefficients and handle operator mixing under RG flow. Keep scheme and basis conventions explicit.

Nonperturbative and Topological Physics.description: Evaluate semiclassical saddle contributions and decay rates. Emphasize qualitative dependence on couplings and scales.
Construct kinks, vortices, or monopoles and analyze topological charges and stability. Contrast classical and quantum aspects.
Relate zero modes to topological invariants and anomalies. Use anomaly matching and inflow as guiding principles.
Organize dynamics in 1/N for SU(N) and identify planar dominance. Discuss qualitative implications for spectra or correlators.
Use Wilson loops to extract static potentials and diagnose confining behavior. Highlight area vs perimeter laws.
Develop self-consistent equations for correlators and perform controlled resummations. Interpret truncations physically.

Curved Spacetime and Semiclassical QFT.description: Define covariant measures and propagators and study curvature effects. Keep to calculable toy geometries or approximations.
Estimate path integrals and transition amplitudes via stationary phase and WKB. Track phases and prefactors carefully.
Use proper-time/heat-kernel techniques to evaluate determinants and effective actions in simple backgrounds.

Conformal, Supersymmetric, and Dual Stru.topic: CFT primaries, descendants, and Ward identities
Conformal, Supersymmetric, and Dual Stru.description: Classify operators by conformal data and compute basic correlators/OPEs. Emphasize Ward identities and dimensional analysis.

Conformal, Supersymmetric, and Dual Stru.topic: N=1,2 superfields and SUSY Ward identities
Conformal, Supersymmetric, and Dual Stru.description: Build superfield actions and derive component interactions. Enforce SUSY Ward identities and multiplet relations.
Match bulk fields to boundary operators and use holographic prescriptions for simple correlators. Keep to introductory, calculable settings.
Discuss topological gauge actions and simple observables (e.g., Wilson lines). Keep to conceptual, computable cases.
